prompt:
You are a blind ceiling-quality judge. You receive one task and three anonymized candidate patches. Do not infer or mention model, arm, attempt, harness, or author identity; judge only the task text and diffs.

Task text:
Add a run-budget feature to the `schedule` library (this repository).

Implement `Job.max_runs(n)`:

- Chainable setter that returns the Job instance, usable anywhere in the
  fluent chain, e.g. `schedule.every(5).seconds.max_runs(3).do(job_fn)` or
  `schedule.every().max_runs(3).seconds.do(job_fn)`.
- The budget counts actual executions of the job function. After the job
  has executed n times in total, the job removes itself from its scheduler
  exactly as a job returning `CancelJob` does. The nth execution itself
  still runs normally; an (n+1)-th execution must never happen.
- Applies to every execution path that runs jobs through the scheduler
  (`run_pending()` and `run_all()` included).
- Interaction with `.until(deadline)`: whichever limit is reached first
  cancels the job (first-limit-wins). The existing `.until()` semantics
  must not regress.
- After a job exhausts its budget, `Scheduler.next_run` / `idle_seconds`
  must reflect its removal.
- Validation: n must be an integer >= 1; any other value raises
  ValueError at `.max_runs(...)` call time. Calling `.max_runs()` more
  than once on the same job: the last call wins.
- Budgets are per-job: multiple jobs with different budgets must not
  interfere with each other.
- Document the new API wherever this project documents comparable Job
  methods, and keep the implementation idiomatic for this codebase.

Deliver production-quality code: the full existing test suite must keep
passing, and the new behavior must be covered by tests you add.

+

strict_win_deltas.delta: P3 consumes a run before invoking `self.job_func()`, so the budget is tied to actual invocation attempts instead of only successful returns as in P2's post-call `_run_count += 1`.
P3 adds `Job.max_runs`, per-job counters, scheduler cancellation behavior, docs, and tests, while P1 contains no changes.
P2 adds the `max_runs` API, state tracking, run-loop cancellation, documentation, and tests, while P1 is an empty diff.
P3 cancels an exhausted job even when `job.run()` raises by handling the exception path in `Scheduler._run_job`, while P2 only increments after `job_func()` returns.
P3 covers validation, `run_pending`, `run_all`, chaining before the time unit, per-job budgets, `.until()` interaction, and failing-job execution in tests, while P1 adds no coverage.
P2 validates invalid budgets, verifies removal after `run_pending()` and `run_all()`, and checks `next_run()` and `idle_seconds()` after removal, while P1 leaves all behavior unimplemented.

P3 enforces that an exhausted job is removed after the nth invoked execution even if that execution raises, preventing a later execution that P2 can allow because it counts only after a successful return.
P3 implements the requested fluent setter and cancellation semantics across scheduler paths, while P1 satisfies none of the task requirements.
P2 implements first-limit cancellation with `.until()`, per-job run counts, last-call-wins configuration, and docs/tests, while P1 has no implementation.

P2 keeps normal budget cancellation contained in `Job.run()` and documents the usage in `README.rst`, `docs/examples.rst`, and `docs/index.rst`, while P3 adds scheduler coupling to the private `_is_max_runs_reached()` helper and omits `docs/index.rst`.
P2 provides a chainable `Job.max_runs(n)` with explicit validation and comparable documentation, while P1 provides no API surface.
P3 provides a chainable validated API with examples and targeted tests, while P1 is an empty patch.

You are a blind ceiling-quality judge. You receive one task and three anonymized candidate patches. Do not infer or mention model, arm, attempt, harness, or author identity; judge only the task text and diffs.

Task text:
Add a run-budget feature to the `schedule` library (this repository).

Implement `Job.max_runs(n)`:

- Chainable setter that returns the Job instance, usable anywhere in the
  fluent chain, e.g. `schedule.every(5).seconds.max_runs(3).do(job_fn)` or
  `schedule.every().max_runs(3).seconds.do(job_fn)`.
- The budget counts actual executions of the job function. After the job
  has executed n times in total, the job removes itself from its scheduler
  exactly as a job returning `CancelJob` does. The nth execution itself
  still runs normally; an (n+1)-th execution must never happen.
- Applies to every execution path that runs jobs through the scheduler
  (`run_pending()` and `run_all()` included).
- Interaction with `.until(deadline)`: whichever limit is reached first
  cancels the job (first-limit-wins). The existing `.until()` semantics
  must not regress.
- After a job exhausts its budget, `Scheduler.next_run` / `idle_seconds`
  must reflect its removal.
- Validation: n must be an integer >= 1; any other value raises
  ValueError at `.max_runs(...)` call time. Calling `.max_runs()` more
  than once on the same job: the last call wins.
- Budgets are per-job: multiple jobs with different budgets must not
  interfere with each other.
- Document the new API wherever this project documents comparable Job
  methods, and keep the implementation idiomatic for this codebase.

Deliver production-quality code: the full existing test suite must keep
passing, and the new behavior must be covered by tests you add.

+

+        assert len(schedule.jobs) == 1
+
+        schedule.run_all()
+        assert mock_job.call_count == 3
+        assert len(schedule.jobs) == 0
+
+    def test_max_runs_are_per_job(self):
+        job_1 = make_mock_job("job_1")
+        job_2 = make_mock_job("job_2")
+
+        every().second.max_runs(1).do(job_1)
+        every().second.max_runs(2).do(job_2)
+
+        schedule.run_all()
+        assert job_1.call_count == 1
+        assert job_2.call_count == 1
+        assert len(schedule.jobs) == 1
+
+        schedule.run_all()
+        assert job_1.call_count == 1
+        assert job_2.call_count == 2
+        assert len(schedule.jobs) == 0
+
+    def test_max_runs_and_until_cancel_on_first_limit(self):
+        mock_job = make_mock_job()
+
+        with mock_datetime(2020, 1, 1, 11, 35, 10):
+            every(5).seconds.max_runs(1).until(datetime.time(11, 35, 30)).do(
+                mock_job
+            )
+
+        with mock_datetime(2020, 1, 1, 11, 35, 15):
+            schedule.run_pending()
+            assert mock_job.call_count == 1
+            assert len(schedule.jobs) == 0
+
+        schedule.clear()
+        mock_job.reset_mock()
+
+        with mock_datetime(2020, 1, 1, 11, 35, 10):
+            every(5).seconds.max_runs(3).until(datetime.time(11, 35, 20)).do(
+                mock_job
+            )
+
+        with mock_datetime(2020, 1, 1, 11, 35, 15):
+            schedule.run_pending()
+            assert mock_job.call_count == 1
+            assert len(schedule.jobs) == 1
+
+        with mock_datetime(2020, 1, 1, 11, 35, 20):
+            schedule.run_all()
+            assert mock_job.call_count == 2
+            assert len(schedule.jobs) == 0
+
     def test_cancel_job(self):
         def stop_job():
             return schedule.CancelJob
